ground_truth:
FINAL REPORT
 EXAMINATION:  CHEST (PA AND LAT)
 
 INDICATION:  History: ___F with smoking history presenting with episodes of
 shortness of breath and chronic cough
 
 TECHNIQUE:  Chest PA and lateral
 
 COMPARISON:  Chest radiograph ___
 
 FINDINGS: 
 
 Heart size is normal.  The mediastinal and hilar contours are normal. The
 pulmonary vasculature is normal. Lungs are clear.  No pleural effusion or
 pneumothorax is seen. There are no acute osseous abnormalities.
 
 IMPRESSION: 
 
 No acute cardiopulmonary abnormality.

FINAL REPORT
 EXAMINATION:  CHEST (PORTABLE AP)
 
 INDICATION:  ___ year old man with IPH, now with fevers, also please check OGT
 placement  // interval change and OGT placement      interval change and OGT
 placement
 
 IMPRESSION: 
 
 In comparison with the study of ___, there again are low lung volumes,
 chronic severe enlargement of the cardiac silhouette, pulmonary vascular
 congestion, and bilateral layering pleural effusions with compressive
 atelectasis at the bases.  Endotracheal tube and left subclavian catheter
 remain in standard position.  The nasogastric tube extends at least to the mid
 to lower body of the stomach, were crosses the lower margin of the image.

FINAL REPORT
 EXAMINATION:  CHEST (PORTABLE AP)
 
 INDICATION:  ___ year old man with NGT placement  // NGT placement      NGT
 placement
 
 IMPRESSION: 
 
 Compared to chest radiographs most recently ___.
 
 ET tube ends less than one cm from the carina, with the chin down.  It should
 be withdrawn 2-3 cm.  Lungs clear.  Heart size normal.  No pleural
 abnormality.
 
 Esophageal drainage tube ends in nondistended stomach.

FINAL REPORT
 HISTORY:  ___-year-old woman with cirrhosis and upper GI bleed, now status post
 massive resuscitation, increased oxygen requirement.  Evaluate pulmonary
 infiltrates.
 
 TECHNIQUE:  Portable AP semi-erect chest radiograph was obtained.
 
 COMPARISON:  Chest radiographs from ___.
 
 FINDINGS:
 
 Continued and unchanged bilateral pulmonary opacification consistent with
 pulmonary edema.  Bibasilar opacities may represent coinciding pneumonia in
 the proper clinical setting.  Small pleural effusions are again seen.  Right
 central venous line, gastric tube, and ET tube are unchanged and are in
 appropriate position. 
 
 IMPRESSION:
 
 Unchanged pulmonary edema.  Basilar opacities may represent pneumonia in the
 proper clinical setting.

FINAL REPORT
 PA AND LATERAL CHEST, ___
 
 COMPARISON:  ___ radiograph.
 
 FINDINGS:  Consolidation in the left lower lobe and lingula has slightly
 worsened.  A subtle opacity in the periphery of the right middle lobe has
 improved.  Bilateral upper lobe pleural and parenchymal scarring appears
 similar to the prior study as well as left lateral pleural thickening.  Healed
 rib and sternal fractures are noted, and unchanged compression deformities are
 evident within the thoracic spine.
 
 IMPRESSION:  Slight worsening of left lower lobe and lingular consolidation
 which has waxed and waned on serial radiographs since ___. 
 Although possibly due to chronic or recurrent aspiration, CT of the chest may
 be helpful to evaluate for possible endobronchial abnormality or
 bronchiectasis if warranted clinically.

FINAL REPORT
 EXAMINATION:  CHEST (PA AND LAT)
 
 INDICATION:  ___ year old man with cabg  // r/o inf, eff
 
 TECHNIQUE:  Single frontal view of the chest
 
 COMPARISON:  ___
 
 IMPRESSION: 
 
 Moderate to severe cardiomegaly is stable.  Widening mediastinum has improved.
 Small bilateral pleural effusions are associated with adjacent atelectasis
 left greater than right.  There is no evident pneumothorax.  Sternal wires are
 aligned

FINAL REPORT
 EXAMINATION:  Is radiograph.
 
 INDICATION:  ___F w/fall, endorsing right chest wall pain.  Assess etiology
 appear
 
 TECHNIQUE:  Chest PA and lateral
 
 COMPARISON:  Chest radiograph ___, ___.
 
 FINDINGS: 
 
 The lungs are mildly hyperinflated and clear.  No pleural effusion or
 pneumothorax.  Heart size, mediastinal contour, and hila are unremarkable.
 
 Limited assessment of the osseous structures are unremarkable.  No displaced
 rib fracture.
 
 IMPRESSION: 
 
 1.  Emphysema or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.
 2.  Although no fracture or other bone abnormality is seen, conventional chest
 radiographs are not appropriate for detection or characterization of chest
 cage lesions. Any focal findings should be clearly marked and imaged with
 either bone detail views or CT scanning.

FINAL REPORT
 INDICATION:  *** CODE CORD *** History: ___M with new intubation  // eval tube
 placement      //*** CODE CORD *** History: ___M with new intubation
 
 TECHNIQUE:
 
 
 Single supine view of the chest
 
 COMPARISON:  None at the time of dictation
 
 FINDINGS: 
 
 An endotracheal tube terminates in appropriate position approximately 4 cm in
 the carina. An NG tube is in the stomach. Cardiac size is top normal. No
 evidence of pneumothorax. No free air.
 
 A large pleural-based opacity projects over the right lung apex and spans
 least 6 x 6 cm. Additional opacities are noted projecting over the left upper
 lobe as well as left lower lobe. Substantial right hilar fullness may be
 secondary lymphadenopathy/mass as well as pulmonary edema.
 
 IMPRESSION: 
 
 Support devices in appropriate position.
 
 Multiple parenchymal masses including a large pleural-based right lung apex
 mass.

FINAL REPORT
 INDICATION:  History of hepatocellular carcinoma and pleural effusion.
 
 COMPARISON:  Chest radiograph dated ___.
 
 TECHNIQUE:  PA and lateral radiographs of the chest.
 
 FINDINGS:  Evaluation of the lateral radiograph is limited due to oblique
 patient positioning.  Apparent mild improvement in a persistent moderate right
 pleural effusion is likely related to differences in patient positioning on
 the current study compared to ___.  Underlying right basilar atelectasis
 is unchanged.  The left lung is grossly clear.  There is no pneumothorax. 
 Right apical scarring appears symmetrical.  The cardiac, mediastinal and hilar
 contours are within normal limits and unchanged.  Embolization coils project
 along the right anterior mediastinum.  A large radiopaque lesion in the right
 hepatic lobe is compatible with the patient's known HCC status post TACE.
 
 IMPRESSION:  Mild apparent improvement in moderate right pleural effusion is
 likely related to patient positioning. Stable right basilar atelectasis.

WET READ: ___ ___ ___ 3:24 AM
  1. Right IJ CVL tip in low SVC.
  2. Mild vascular congestion.
  3.  New small retrocardiac opacity with small left pleural effusion is
  worrisome for pneumonia in the appropriate clinical setting.
  
 ______________________________________________________________________________
                                 FINAL REPORT
 EXAMINATION:  Chest radiograph.
 
 INDICATION:  ___F with RIJ CVL.  Assess line placement
 
 TECHNIQUE:  Chest PA and lateral
 
 COMPARISON:  Chest radiograph ___.  CT chest without contrast ___.
 
 FINDINGS: 
 
 The lungs are hypoinflated with crowding of vasculature, mild vascular
 congestion, and bibasilar atelectasis.  Heterogeneous retrocardiac opacity is
 present.  There is a new small left pleural effusion.  No right pleural
 effusion.  Heart size is likely accentuated due to low lung volumes and
 patient positioning.  Mediastinal contour and hila are otherwise unremarkable.
 
 Right IJ CVL tip in low SVC.
 
 IMPRESSION: 
 
 1.  Right IJ CVL tip in low SVC.
 2.  Mild vascular congestion.
 3.  New small retrocardiac opacity with small left pleural effusion is
 worrisome for pneumonia in the appropriate clinical setting.

FINAL REPORT
 HISTORY:  History of breast cancer with brain metastases status post
 resection.  Evaluate for pneumonia.
 
 COMPARISON:  Prior chest radiographs from ___ and ___.  
 
 TECHNIQUE:  PA and lateral chest radiogrpahs. 
 
 FINDINGS:
 
 The cardiomediastinal and hilar contours are within normal limits.  There is
 mild calcification of the aortic knob.  Lungs are well expanded. There is mild
 atelectasis at the left lung base. There is no focal consolidation, pleural
 effusion or pneumothorax.  
 
 IMPRESSION:
 
 No acute cardiopulmonary process.

FINAL REPORT
 INDICATION:  History: ___M with chest pain  // ? pna
 
 COMPARISON:  None.
 
 IMPRESSION: 
 
 Cardiomediastinal silhouette is within normal limits.  Lungs are grossly clear
 without focal consolidation or pulmonary edema.  On the lateral view, there is
 blunting of the posterior CP angle on the left suggestive of a small pleural
 effusion. There are no pneumothoraces.

FINAL REPORT
 EXAMINATION:  CHEST RADIOGRAPHS
 
 INDICATION:  Shortness of breath.
 
 TECHNIQUE:  Chest, PA and lateral.
 
 COMPARISON:  ___.
 
 FINDINGS: 
 
 The heart is borderline in size.  The mediastinal and cardiac contours appear
 stable.  There is new bilateral hilar fullness with vascular engorgement
 including indistinct margins, most consistent with mild to moderate pulmonary
 edema.  Fissures are thickened.  There is no definite pleural effusion or
 pneumothorax.
 
 IMPRESSION: 
 
 Findings consistent with pulmonary edema.

FINAL REPORT
 TYPE OF EXAMINATION:  CHEST PA AND LATERAL.
 
 HISTORY:  ___-year-old male patient with hypoxia trying to get into
 detoxification program, history of ethanol, evaluate for possible pneumonia.
 
 FINDINGS:  PA and lateral chest views were obtained with patient in upright
 position.  Analysis is performed in direct comparison with the next preceding
 AP and lateral chest examination ___ ___.  Previously identified mild
 cardiac enlargement has regressed and presently the cardiac contours are
 within normal limits.  The thoracic aorta is mildly widened and elongated but
 there are no local suspicious contour abnormalities.  The pulmonary
 vasculature is not congested.  No evidence of acute or chronic parenchymal
 infiltrates are present and the lateral and posterior pleural sinuses are
 free.  The patient's inability to elevate arms somewhat detract from the image
 quality on the lateral view but again no acute abnormality is identified.  In
 comparison with the next previous examination at that time existing mild
 congestive pulmonary pattern has normalized.
 
 IMPRESSION:  No residuals of previously identified questionable CHF. 
 Presently, the chest findings are within normal limits.

FINAL REPORT
 EXAMINATION:   CHEST (PA AND LAT)
 
 INDICATION:  ___ year old man with APLA syndrome with multiple h/o
 thromboembolic events, now with fever.  Evaluate for pneumonia.
 
 TECHNIQUE:  Chest PA and lateral
 
 COMPARISON:  Chest radiograph from ___ and chest CTA of ___.
 
 FINDINGS: 
 
 Left chest pacemaker with leads projecting to the a right ventricle is
 unchanged.  Cardiomediastinal silhouettes are normal.  Lung volumes are lower
 causing bronchovascular crowding.  No focal consolidation, pleural effusion,
 or pneumothorax.
 
 IMPRESSION: 
 
 Lower lung volumes, without focal consolidation concerning for pneumonia.

FINAL REPORT
 EXAMINATION:  CHEST (PA AND LAT)
 
 INDICATION:  ___ year old woman with worsening cough x 4 days. Hx asthma.  //
 Evaluate for abnormalities      CHRONIC COUGH W/CONGESTION AND SOME SOB PER PT
 4 DAYS R/O ABNORMALITY
 
 IMPRESSION: 
 
 In comparison with the study of ___, there is little change.  Again
 there is hyperexpansion of the lungs with flattening hemidiaphragms consistent
 with chronic pulmonary disease.  No acute pneumonia, vascular congestion, or
 pleural effusion.

FINAL REPORT
 CHEST RADIOGRAPH
 
 INDICATION:  Recurrent pneumonia, evaluation of interval change.
 
 COMPARISON:  ___.
 
 FINDINGS:  As compared to the previous radiograph, the pre-existing left lung
 peripheral opacity as well as two very subtle apical right opacities have
 almost completely resolved.  No opacities have newly occurred.  No pleural
 effusions.  Normal size of the cardiac silhouette.  Normal hilar and
 mediastinal structures.

FINAL REPORT
 EXAMINATION:  CHEST (PORTABLE AP)
 
 INDICATION:  ___ year old man with tachypnea and desaturations  // SOB,
 desaturation      SOB, desaturation
 
 COMPARISON:  Chest radiographs ___ through ___.
 
 IMPRESSION: 
 
 Right lower lobe collapse has not improved. Right middle lobe volume loss
 indicates subtotal atelectasis, also unchanged. Atelectasis explains continued
 hypoxia.  Left lung is now clear previous vascular congestion. Heart size
 normal.
 
 Nasogastric tube passes to the distal portion of a nondistended stomach. No
 pneumothorax. Right pleural effusion is presumed, secondary to be atelectasis,
 but not substantial. The left.

FINAL REPORT
 HISTORY:  Possible pneumonia.
 
 FINDINGS:  In comparison with the study of ___, there is little interval
 change.  There are areas of asymmetric opacification at the right base and in
 the left mid lung zone, which could reflect multifocal pneumonia.  Otherwise,
 little change.

FINAL REPORT
 EXAMINATION:  CHEST (PORTABLE AP)
 
 INDICATION:  ___ year old woman with CAD s/p cath today, now with post-cath
 shortness of breath and wheezing  // ? any evidence of pulmonary edema or new
 opacity
 
 COMPARISON:  ___.
 
 IMPRESSION: 
 
 As compared to the previous radiograph, no relevant change is noted.  Moderate
 cardiomegaly.  No pulmonary edema.  No pleural effusions.  No pneumonia.
